title: Micro-Authorization Digital Goods Probe with Enumerated Card Series
narrative: Fraudster validates stolen card portfolio using single $0.99-$1.49 charges at streaming service or digital content merchant (ProductCD='C' or 'R'). Pattern shows sequential card1 values suggesting enumerated PANs (e.g., 4532...1001, 4532...1002, 4532...1003) all hitting same MID within 90-180 seconds. Transactions originate from residential proxy network with dist1 values showing 800-2400km separation from cardholder billing addresses. Email domain rotates through temporary providers (P_emaildomain in 'guerrillamail.com', 'tempmail.net', '10minutemail.com'). Successful validations (no decline) are immediately followed by larger purchases at different merchants within 15-45 minutes. AVS and CVV match rates exceed 85% indicating full card data possession, not just card numbers.
indicators: TransactionAmt fixed at $0.99-$1.49 across 8+ transactions in 90-180 second window
card1 values show sequential enumeration pattern (last 3-4 digits increment by 1-2)
All transactions share identical ProductCD='C' or 'R' and same merchant ID
dist1 shows 800-2400km distance between transaction and cardholder address
P_emaildomain concentrates in 3-4 temporary email providers across card series
card1_txn_count_24h = 1 for each card (first-time usage at this merchant)
85%+ AVS_match and CVV_match rates indicating full card data availability
summary: Sequential card numbers (e.g., ending in 1001, 1002, 1003) all attempt small $0.99-$1.49 digital purchases at the same merchant within 90-180 seconds, originating from locations 800+ km from cardholders' billing addresses with temporary email domains.
reasoning: Fraudsters test stolen card databases by making tiny charges that cardholders rarely notice, using sequential card numbers to efficiently validate which cards are still active before moving to high-value fraud at different merchants.
indicator_explanations: All purchase amounts locked at micro-transaction levels to stay under fraud alerts
Card numbers follow sequential patterns suggesting automated enumeration of stolen card ranges
Every transaction hits the same digital goods merchant with identical product codes
Purchases originate from locations hundreds of miles from the cardholder's billing address
Email addresses cluster among a few throwaway domains across the card batch
Each card shows first-ever activity at this merchant indicating fresh testing
High verification match rates prove attacker has complete card details including CVV
